intro: One paragraph from Mary Grande's 1989 interview, transformed at each stage of the pipeline. The paragraph — a child catching blood at a pig slaughter — appears in the right place at every step.

stages.name: Raw text
stages.summary: A paragraph from the transcript, untouched.

data.text: I had to help with, when they killed the pigs I had to catch the blood. I didn't like it, but that was part of my job.

stages.name: StructureDoc
stages.summary: The LLM places this paragraph inside a section it has named, with a descriptive gist.

data.label: S2 · Slaughter and child labor
data.gist: The animals are pets and food at the same time. The speaker moves from enjoying the animals to describing their slaughter, then to her own reluctant participation as a child.
data.meta: char 2680–3380 · gists are descriptive only, no analytic language

stages.name: Excerpt
stages.summary: The paragraph is flattened to an excerpt with a section anchor and a position.

data.label: §S2 · position 5 of 6
data.text: I had to help with, when they killed the pigs I had to catch the blood. I didn't like it, but that was part of my job.
data.meta: 1 paragraph · the coder sees section + position + excerpt, never a floating paragraph

stages.name: Codes (N=2)
stages.summary: Two personas — different reading sensibilities (prompt variants), not different code types. Each emits a semantic code and, where warranted, a latent one; here A's semantic and B's latent readings are shown. Both anchored to the same quote; both written with a chain-of-thought rationale.

stages.name: Verify span
stages.summary: The exact-match gate. If the quote doesn't appear verbatim in the source, the code is quarantined, not stored.

data.quote: I had to help with, when they killed the pigs I had to catch the blood. I didn't like it, but that was part of my job.

stages.name: Conciliator: create vs merge
stages.summary: Each new code is compared against the whole current codebook. The conciliator decides create, merge, or minority — with a written rationale. Divergent readings are kept, not voted out.

data.label: Friction between obligation and reluctance
data.rationale: Persona B's latent code arrives at a codebook that already holds Persona A's 'Child participation in slaughter' from the same excerpt. They are not duplicates: A names what happened, B names what the sentence does — holds duty and aversion in one breath. Collapsing them would erase the reading RTA is designed to preserve. Both enter the codebook; the shared span links them.

stages.name: Theme
stages.summary: A single theorist pass over the final codebook writes a central-concept statement — a claim, not a category label.

data.label: Childhood inside the agrarian household
data.statement: Children in this household learn adulthood through tasks that mix obligation with reluctance; the 'I had to / I didn't like it / part of my job' structure is the moment where normalization and resistance meet in a single sentence, and the pet cow that is also dinner is the same meeting at the level of the household's relation to its animals.
